mnemonics: CATCH ORDER: Catch the Kids Before the Parents (Base classes last)
THROW WHAT YOU KNOW: A derived class object is-a base class object, so it can be thrown where a base class handler exists.
SLICE & CATCH: Throwing by value can cause object slicing; throw by pointer/reference to preserve the derived type.
keyPoints: Exception handlers are matched in the order they appear. Always catch derived class exceptions before base class exceptions.
A handler for a base class will also catch exceptions of derived classes due to the 'is-a' relationship.
If you throw an exception object by value, it may be sliced when caught by a base class handler. Throwing by pointer or reference avoids this.
commonMistakes: Writing catch blocks for the base class before the derived class, making the derived handler unreachable.
Attempting to catch an exception by a derived class reference after it has been sliced by being caught by a base class value.
Forgetting that a catch(...) block (catch-all) must be the last handler, as it will catch any exception.
